messages.text:
Here is an update on the PCO. We hit our targets. The cure maxed out at 85%  at our current formulation, so when we reach 100%, we should have a stiffer PCO that is less extensible, but we have a lot of room to play in the composition space based on our mechanical property data.

thank you, <@U0127BGJ3U5>, for making the document look legible. (I'm not good at making things pretty).

If I am missing anything, please chime in <@U012M8NDV3K> to help guide everyone if I am not clear.
The target was 15MPa tensile strength and at least 15% elongation

<@U06F97BPQJU>, <@U012ETLV6NQ> has managed to develop what I think without question counts as an MVP. We've got samples that we can ship. The question before sending is do we ship them the mixture of the two monomers (soybean mix and CHXMA), or do we ship them separately? If you trust the polyset folks, I would recommend sending them separately, that gives them the opportunity to play with other compositions as they start to think around the properties that we aren't able to test (i.e. adhesion to concrete).

For reference, I screenshotted the resin/primer specifications. The system Baker developed will serve as a 2 in 1 primer and resin formulation.
1. Viscosity: <150 cps
2. Flash point: >= 180 °F
3. Specific gravity will be <1, so technically this is outside of the spec, but I would anticipate that being fine. If it would stop us from being able to be successful, we can alter density
4. Elongation = 45% 
5. We don't know about adhesion to concrete 
Future developments would include:
1. increasing the bio/soy content
2. reducing/eliminating VOC
    a. Both the primer and the resin use volatile monomers. CHXMA is lower volatility, lower flammability, and lower toxicity, but is still considered a VOC. There are tricks to eliminate that.
Michael, do me a favor and talk to Austin…
